data.title: AAON: Impacted By Operational Challenges
data.description:
AAON Inc. (NASDAQ:AAON) has delivered an outstanding performance over the last decade (20.1% CAGR), but the recent operational challenges have driven a 50% drawdown in its stock price.

Despite the strong demand for data center equipment, the refrigerant transition and the recent ERP implementation have significantly disrupted AAON's manufacturing and supply operations.

The roll-out of the new ERP system didn't go as expected, and due to the significant fixed costs and operating leverage, margins declined sharply in the most recent quarter, causing a 69.4% Y/Y decline in EPS.

However, the demand is still strong, and backlog anticipates a better second half of the year since new orders already include the price increases implemented in January and March.

In this article, in addition to reviewing the impacts of the most recent developments, I update AAON's valuation model to determine if the stock presents an attractive buying opportunity.

AAON Overview

AAON is a $6.6B market cap company that designs, manufactures, and sells high-quality semi-custom HVAC equipment for commercial and industrial clients.

Since its foundation in 1988 by Norman H. Asbjornson (age 89), who serves as a director and retains 17.1% ownership, AAON has expanded its manufacturing facilities to over 4M square feet and 4,800 employees.

AAON currently holds a 6% market share in the non-residential HVAC market, but the 2021 acquisition of BASX Solutions significantly expanded its addressable market.

The company entered the fast-growing data center market at the right moment and applied the same strategy that has supported its growth for decades: deliver highly customizable products with lower total cost of ownership (fewer repairs needed and energy-efficient) and premium customer service.

As a result, BASX's backlog has grown from less than $100M three years ago to $623M in the most recent quarter, leading to significant investments in expanding the manufacturing facilities.

Although AAON faces competition from Lennox International Inc. (LII), Carrier Global Corp. (CARR), and Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT), among others, the industry is consolidated among a few large players.

And despite the limited pricing power, there are some barriers to entry, such as achieving sufficient scale in the manufacturing process, innovation, intellectual property, and R&D capabilities.

To differentiate itself, AAON has heavily invested in innovation and delivering the highest-quality products at a premium and is now focused on narrowing the pricing gap.

Impacted By Operational Challenges

Since January 2025, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has restricted the use of certain gases in HVAC equipment, and as a result, AAON has not only incurred higher costs to update its products, but the transition has also caused significant operational disruptions.

Although AAON started selling new equipment a year ahead of the deadline and was only accepting orders for new equipment since Q4 2024, the industry suffered from significant supply chain bottlenecks.

In addition to the supply chain disruptions, which ended up impacting manufacturing operations, AAON had to update the building code and re-certify certain components.

The challenge is the 454B transition is the first in the history of the HVAC industry that had an EPA-driven environmental driver for why we're changing refrigerants, but also an implication to the building code.

And so the fact that this refrigerant is an A2L, a mildly favorable refrigerant, we had to modify the building code to allow the use of this refrigerant.

Source: Matthew J. Tobolski (CEO) - 2025 Sidoti Small-Cap Conference.

And once the refrigerant transition seemed to be approaching its end, the roll-out of a new ERP system made things worse.

With the recent expansion, executives considered that the business management systems needed an upgrade to support future growth and started implementing a new SAP system in April 2025 in the Longview, TX, facility (the second largest facility after Tulsa, OK).

However, the roll-out didn't go as expected, and the production constraints were higher than anticipated.

The rationale behind starting in Longview was that this facility manufactures AAON-branded products, BASX equipment, and coils, so future roll-outs in other facilities would be easier to implement.

But the problem was that Longview produces the majority of coils used in the Tulsa facility, and while the management was expecting to compensate for the production decline with two external suppliers, they couldn't meet the demand.

While the BASX segment was not significantly impacted, gross margins of AAON-branded products declined from 37.2% a year ago to 27.5% and from 41.9% to 22% in coil products.

After a significant decline in production and gross margins during April, the situation improved throughout the quarter and July, and with the Longview experience, the implementation in other facilities should be smoother, but I expect some short-term pressure on gross margins considering that the ERP system isn't yet implemented in Tulsa.

The management revised its guidance to factor in the implementation of the ERP system in other facilities and is now expecting low-teens revenue growth (down from mid-to-high teens) and 28-29% gross margins (down from 33%) in FY 2025.

Backlog at All-Time Highs

While the company is struggling to supply some of the orders, the demand is still strong, and the backlog keeps increasing.

In the BASX segment, the backlog increased by 58% Y/Y, but the increase was even higher in AAON products (93.4% Y/Y) as a result of the operational challenges and the delayed revenues.

AAON Oklahoma revenues declined by 18% Y/Y in Q2 2025, but I view the situation as a revenue deferral since once production rebounds, revenues and margins will recover.

Given AAON's fixed costs and operating leverage, a decline in revenues translates into lower margins, which has led to a substantial EPS estimate revision (down from $2.94 in January 2025 to the current $1.36).

However, the 33% Y/Y EPS decline will quickly recover in 2026 and 2027 if there is no significant economic slowdown and the demand from data centers remains strong.

Production levels have already improved in recent months, and AAON will now benefit from the tailwind of price increases.

Until now, most of the production has been for orders received last year, but the company implemented a 3% price increase in January and another 6% tariff surcharge in March, which should help offset higher production costs.

The Evolving Data Center Demand

The reason why AAON has traded between 40x and 60x LTM EPS in recent years is due to the strong demand from data centers, which is key to justify these lousy multiples for an HVAC company.

Given the importance of this segment, I believe it is highly important to correctly assess forward demand for AI infrastructure, and for now, it's not showing any weakness.

During the most recent quarter, sales to data centers increased by 127%, and the management now expects a 40% revenue growth for the BASX segment during 2025, which is above the 38% CAGR delivered in the three previous years.

In my view, AAON is perfectly positioned to benefit from the rapidly growing demand for data center cooling solutions, which is reflected in the recently announced partnership with Applied Digital (APLD), an AI data center developer.

Data center demand is not just growing at a fast pace, but it is also evolving quickly, and here is where I believe AAON can leverage its competitive advantage.

Since the company produces fully-custom and semi-custom equipment, it can quickly adapt to its customers' needs, as it has done in recent years by increasing the production of liquid cooling solutions.

When looking at independent research, most sources forecast around a 15% CAGR in data center capacity over the next 5 years, and McKinsey & Co. projects a 21% CAGR.

Even when adding an additional 3-4% growth from price increases, the industry expected growth is below AAON's management expectations of 40% revenue growth from BASX products over the next 3 years, and that is why I take a conservative approach in my estimates.

Valuation

While I agree on AAON's mid-single-digit growth expectations in the legacy segment, I forecast lower revenue growth in BASX products.

As growth rates from data centers diminish over time, I expect a valuation multiple contraction to 30x LTM EPS, which is in line with AAON's valuation before the AI boom.

Using an 8.5% discount rate (9% cost of equity, 6.3% cost of debt, and 0.94 beta) and a 4% growth in perpetuity (inflation), AAON's stock is fairly priced at $80 per share.

In my base case scenario, assuming a steady decline in growth rates and the valuation multiple, the risk/reward situation doesn't seem compelling enough to me since there is no margin of safety, and the stock would deliver an 87.7% CAGR going forward.

However, if BASX grows at 40% as expected by the management and margins expand due to scale efficiencies, the stock would deliver above-average returns.

For now, I view AAON's stock as a hold, but any stock price decline below $80 would make it an attractive buy.

Risks

Considering that AAON's investment thesis is built around the strong demand for BASX products, the most obvious risk would be a substantial slowdown in AI infrastructure spending.

At the moment, most of the demand for data center cooling equipment is from the large hyperscalers, which have the capital to embark on this journey.

Nonetheless, if, after a few years of high spending, they realize that the benefits are not as expected, they could reduce their investments in AI infrastructure.

Also, if a recession were to hit the economy in the near future, hyperscalers could delay some projects, which would reduce AAON's fair value.

The other risk I would highlight is related to AAON's financials, in particular, the constant need to reinvest in CapEx.

AAON could fit well in some portfolios seeking exposure to industrials, but it is important to take into account that the company needs to always reinvest in R&D to remain competitive.

While expanding manufacturing operations is necessary to meet the increasing demand and support AAON's competitive advantage, the high CapEx represents a drag on free cash flow.

Note: The reinvestment rate is calculated by dividing capital expenditures plus change in net working capital by operating cash flow.

Conclusion: Hold

The recent headwinds from the ERP implementation and the refrigerant transition have significantly impacted AAON's manufacturing operations and, as a result, bottom-line figures.

However, these are one-time events, and I expect the situation to start improving in Q3 and accelerate during 2026.

From a long-term perspective, I expect AAON to keep growing its business due to the superior value proposition and the strong demand for data center equipment, but the stock might have already priced most of it.

AAON's stock is trading above its fair value of $80 per share in a base case scenario, and I view it as a hold.

data.title: GE Vernova: Riding The Global AI Power Surge With Margin Upside Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) has had a sensational run this year, with its shares rising roughly 85% YTD, which compares favorably with the S&P 500's 9% increase in value in the same period. Ever since its spin-off from the General Electric conglomerate in April 2024, the company has found itself in the middle of the AI momentum trade, pushing up not only hyperscaler and semiconductor stocks but also derivative plays like the power generation sector. In the upcoming 'Age of Electricity', supply has to keep up with roaring demand to keep growth on track. In this article, I will explain to you why I believe that GE Vernova is in an outstanding position to deliver that supply through its various offerings for the energy sector and will continue providing shareholder value in the process. With that said, let's dive right in!

Thesis

GE Vernova will benefit directly from the rapidly growing electricity demand around the world. With its broad footprint within the power generation industry through offerings in gas, wind, and nuclear, the company is in a prime position to benefit from trends like the AI data center build-out, growing electric vehicle adoption, and industrial electrification. Through its Electrification segment, the firm is meeting high demand for grid modernization in the U.S. to set up the infrastructure necessary for withstanding the increasing load. With a large backlog of $129 billion of orders yet to be delivered and a growing amount of slot reservations for its gas turbines, GE Vernova can assert pricing power and therefore increase margins. With its 2028 targets for EBITDA margins already in sight, I expect the company to raise its outlook for the medium term within its Power and Electrification segments, further underscoring improving operational excellence since going public.

Q2 2025 Results

GE Vernova delivered another quarter of excellent results when the company released its Q2 2025 financials on July 23.

Revenues increased +12% y/y to $9.1bn, with equipment sales showing particularly strong growth at +18% y/y, while services revenues were up +6% y/y.

The adjusted EBITDA rose by +25% y/y to $770m, coming with a margin expansion of 210bps to 8.5%.

On a segment level, power revenues increased +9% y/y with margins at 16.4%; wind revenues were also up +9% y/y with the segment still making losses; and electrification revenues were the strongest with +20% growth y/y and margins at 14.6%.

New orders reached $12.4 billion, up +4% y/y, with equipment orders growing at +5% y/y. This was led by orders in the Power segment at +44% y/y, while Wind orders declined by -5% y/y.

The company's total backlog now sits at $129bn, with the equipment backlog growing by $5bn in the quarter and the services backlog adding $1bn to the total.

The company also raised its full-year guidance in revenue, free cash flow, and margins.

A Pure Play On Global Electricity Demand

GE Vernova sits at the center of exploding global electricity demand. Consumption is expected to increase by an average of 4% annually through 2027, driven primarily by rising industrial electrification, the continued adoption of electric vehicles, the need for air conditioning, and most importantly, data centers. This is noticeably higher than the 2.6% CAGR from 2015-2023, with 2024 marking a particularly strong year with growth of 4.4%. While certain regions, such as China (5.7% forecast for 2026) or India (6.6% forecast for 2026), are growing demand at above-average rates due to structural economic catch-up potential, the U.S. will still show really strong growth over the long term, with recent estimates suggesting 2% annual growth through 2050, which would equal a 50% rise over current levels.

As the chart below shows, coal has been on a steady decline in terms of its share in the overall electricity generation mix in the western world. Gas has stepped in as a bridge solution, offering reliable base load and peak capabilities that renewable energy sources aren't able to provide.

If the pace of the data center buildout continues to be as high as right now, the electricity demand it creates would more than double by 2030, equivalent to Japan's total power consumption today. For 2024, electricity consumption from data centers was estimated to be around 1.5% of global power consumption. This could rise to 2.6% by 2035 on a global level, according to the IEA, and as high as 12% in the U.S. by 2028.

The recent announcement of America's AI Action Plan in July, which includes deregulations to speed up AI development, streamlining data center and semiconductor fab permissions, as well as investing in resilient grid infrastructure, is another action that demonstrates how high of a priority artificial intelligence has become.

As hyperscalers are seemingly not stopping to pour money into data center expansions, as can be seen in the table above, the future outlook for GE Vernova as a key supplier of energy generation equipment could not be more promising.

As a bonus to the thesis, we have to take the nuclear business into consideration. In May, GE Vernova announced that it received approval from the Province of Ontario to proceed with the deployment of the first small modular reactor (SMR) in the Western world through its alliance with Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY). Additionally, Swedish utility Vattenfall announced this week that it selected GE Vernova and Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) as candidates to supply a series of SMRs as Sweden's electricity demand is about to double over the next two decades. Small modular reactors come with the advantage of modular factory production, shorter build times, flexible siting in remote or smaller locations, as well as overall cost reductions due to standardized parts. As countries around the world are trying to achieve emission targets, SMRs offer an intriguing opportunity for GE Vernova to capture.

Power and Electrification Driving Margin Upside

At its 2024 Investor Day, GE Vernova shared its financial outlook for the end of 2028. A part of that was their EBITDA margin guidance of 16% for the Power and Electrification segments as well as a 10% target for the Wind business.

If we take a look at the recent financial results that I discussed above, we are already exceeding this target in the Power segment and are close to getting there with Electrification. As a consequence, I expect GE Vernova to raise its targets at their upcoming investor day in December 2025, creating room for sustained positive investor sentiment. What gives me confidence to assume that their margins will continue trending higher is the high amount of slot reservations in the gas power business as well as a favorable mix shift to profitable service offerings. As you can see in the chart below, slot reservations increased to 25GW in the quarter.

Customers are reserving production slots for gas turbines years in advance to make sure that they are receiving the equipment they need when they need it. This is necessary because demand for these turbines has gone up significantly in recent years, driven by accelerated electrification trends and data center build-outs. But GE Vernova's production capacity is limited, currently sitting at around 50 turbines per year. Even with the firm being committed to increase that capacity to 70 to 80 turbines annually, demand will still outweigh supply. As a consequence, rising slot reservations are a leading indicator of pricing power for GE Vernova. Because prices are not yet set with reservations, customers are prioritizing delivery guarantees over pricing, giving GE Vernova the ability to negotiate based on demand. If this trend of high demand for their products continues, the backlog will not just grow in size but also in value as higher margin orders are converted from reservations.

Talking about the assumption of a favorable mix shift, based on recent earnings results, the total backlog is divided into $50bn in equipment orders and $79bn in service orders. This equates to equipment orders taking up 38.8% of the total backlog, while service orders are occupying a 61.2% share. This shows that services will rise in their relative importance to the company's top line in the future. It is commonly assumed that services are higher in terms of profitability than equipment sales. Since GE Vernova does not release separate margin figures for the equipment and services businesses, we can make an educated guess at how these two compare to each other. Service revenues make up roughly 68% of total Power revenues, with equipment sales representing the remaining 32%. With overall Power EBITDA margins currently sitting at around 16%, I estimate service margins to be at 17-18% while equipment margins are slightly lower at 13-14%. With higher pricing over the next few years driving equipment margins higher and services growing into a 70% share of segment revenues, I could see the company achieving Power margins of 18-20%.

For the electrification segment, I expect the effect to be similar, but to a lesser extent. Production capacities for switchgear and transformers are also constrained, with delivery times of up to 3 years. On top of that, since the segment is currently the fastest-growing business for the company, it taking up a larger share of the overall revenue pie will also result in improving margins, as it offers profitability metrics comparable to those of the Power segment. However, service revenues are roughly 30% of total segment sales, which is considerably lower than the 65-70% in Power. Hence, I believe that the effect of a shift towards more service revenues is going to be a lesser tailwind for Electrification than it is for Power. Overall, I expect margins for the segment to land in the range of 16-18% for the estimation period, still exceeding the initial guidance that GE Vernova released at their Investor Day.

For the Wind segment, I believe that the target of 10% EBITDA margins by 2028 is adequate considering the problems the company is facing here. As old margin-dilutive offshore projects leave the firm's backlog, profitability should go up after. The current strategy for Wind is far more selective, especially in offshore. GE Vernova is committed to only accepting profitable orders in the future, essentially going with a 'value over volume' approach. With the expectation that these unprofitable offshore projects are about to be completed by the end of 2026, an improvement of Wind margins could be on the horizon. In my view, the market is currently pricing in Wind as a drag on overall earnings as it has been a losing segment for quite some time now. As a contrarian view, I look at Wind as a potential catalyst for future margin upside with healthy growth going forward.

Valuation

To determine whether GE Vernova is a good investment right now, we have to take a look at the relative valuation versus industry peers.

EV/EBITDA (FWD) EBITDA Margin (TTM)
GE Vernova 47.14 6.61%
Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF) 18.10 6.28%
Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (OTCPK:MHVYF) 22.05 9.79%
Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY) 12.39 14.41%
Average 24.92 9.27%

Using an EV/EBITDA relative valuation method, I calculated the average multiple for the peer group of power equipment manufacturers in the gas, wind, and grid industries, which is 24.92. This indicates a relative overvaluation of GE Vernova's shares at the moment. Despite the strong fundamental growth drivers and attractive margin upside case, I recognize that shares have had a really strong run since going public last year. As a result, I would hold on to my position if I were a shareholder and wait for pullbacks to add to the position if attractive opportunities arise.

Risks to the Thesis

The primary risk associated with an investment in GE Vernova is the relatively high valuation. In my opinion, the fundamental growth drivers are extremely strong, and the prospect of margin expansion through pricing power and a higher share of service revenues is plausible. However, I believe that a lot of future growth is already reflected in the stock price after a sensational run since the spin-off in April of 2024. This makes the stock vulnerable to potential drawdowns if the overall market sentiment shifts to a risk-off phase, since GE Vernova usually trades with high positive correlations to the large technology firms. Another potential risk could be that hyperscalers are slowing down on their CAPEX spending habits due to decreasing returns on their investments. This would impact GE Vernova in a material way as a key supplier of power generation equipment for data centers. However, currently all signs are showing continued capacity investments at very high levels. Last but not least, on a segment level, further struggles in the Wind business could turn out to be a drag on an otherwise exceptional business, capping margin upside with ongoing losses if profitability cannot be turned around in offshore projects.

Summary

GE Vernova has transformed itself into one of the most appealing infrastructure plays in the market today, profiting from the global boom in electrification and artificial intelligence. With a huge backlog of $129bn, growing slot reservations, and an above 60% share of high-margin service offerings, the company offers strong pricing power and high cash flow visibility. With Power and Electrification segments already approaching the guided 2028 targets for margins, a raise at their next Investor Day seems likely. With secular tailwinds like the structurally rising demand for electricity as well as political support through initiatives like 'America's AI Action Plan', GE Vernova is well positioned to capture its share of that opportunity. With that said, as shares have experienced an outstanding run for the past 16 months, the valuation appears a little bit stretched at the moment. As a consequence, I initiate coverage on GE Vernova with a Hold rating.

data.title: Paramount Resources: Replacing Lost Production
data.description:
Paramount Resources (OTCPK:PRMRF) (TSX:POU:CA) reported that the processing plant being built to handle some of its production was completed early. With an earlier startup, the guidance for the fiscal year changed a bit. This is a small bit of encouragement for a company that appears to be on its way to again grow some very valuable production.

Last Article

The last article noted that Paramount was heading back in the direction of dry gas with the Sinclair discovery. This particular acreage would appear to be unusually profitable for dry gas production.

However, the preliminary results coming in from the rich gas production appear to be every bit as competitive (as of the second quarter). These announced goals can be very sensitive to commodity prices and perceived future market conditions. It is therefore quite possible that "all of the above" will be developed to give the company some ability to shift the production mix as needed.

I just love choices between money and more money. That seems to be the case here.

Earnings

(Note: Paramount Resources is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

A lot of traditional measures of progress (like comparing the second quarter to the first quarter or even the previous fiscal year) are going to be meaningless as the company previously sold a big chunk of production to Ovintiv (OVV). That deal closed in the first quarter as was previously reported while resulting in a big gain that is shown for the first quarter. Obviously, this result, while good, is not something that makes for a relevant comparison.

As part of the deal there was a special dividend, share repurchases, and of course a fair amount of cash retained to get the business going again with the remaining leases.

It is easy to see from the trend report the effect that the sale of assets to Ovintiv had on the company's production. There are plenty of company reports on the effect of the sale throughout the company website for interested readers.

The big deal here is that a sizable cash balance is to be spent in the current fiscal year to get production to very roughly 50,000 BOED by the first quarter of the next fiscal year. Much depends upon well results and operations in general. But management appears to have done the due diligence necessary to bring this rather large project to a successful conclusion. This goal is likely far more important than the quarterly earnings progress, as this fiscal year will have a lot of expenditures that will not repeat at the current levels for some time to come.

The thing to watch with any rapid startup is the profitability and potentially the free cash flow because in the next fiscal year, this company will be living within that free cash flow that gets produced.

This company has a history of both growing production while paying a dividend and sometimes repurchasing shares as well. It is therefore very likely that management has again found very profitable production.

Shareholder Returns

This strategy takes variable returns to a new level. This is one of very few companies that makes the old strategy work under new conditions by finding extremely profitable acreage to operate.

Now, the first thing to consider is that the big distribution is a once every several years event. A lot of companies used to do this using borrowing. But the debt market has put a stop to that. Now, above average profitability leases are the prime requirement. It is therefore no surprise that many legitimate "asset growth" artists have now left the industry for greener pastures.

But when assets can grow without needing much debt, as is the case here, then this strategy is available. When the business can grow while paying a dividend and buying back shares with a debt free balance sheet, it is even better for the company.

Since the cycle is just starting, then about all that is reasonable to expect is a nominal dividend until production reaches a level management likely has as a goal.

It is almost a sure bet that a few years down the road, production will again be sold to restart the process. About the only thing that may stop it is the next generation of this family run corporation that may have potentially a different idea. But let's wait until we get there first.

Summary

Paramount Resources is a very unusual growth and total return story. The strategy used here was far more common when companies could use generous helpings of debt along the way.

In the immediate future, there is a positive revision in guidance. But even if the worst were to happen, the usually debt-free balance sheet allows this company as many chances as it needs to be successful.

The share repurchase program will likely be put on hold (in terms of meaningful amounts), and the dividend will be paid at a very low level until production is higher than it is now.

This management typically emphasizes production growth at the start of a new growth cycle. That would appear to be the case right now. The debt-free balance sheet combined with the fact that management does not do "road shows" often means that this stock is undervalued. That undervalued situation is what makes the periodic asset sales a bonanza for shareholders.

We still need to see results coming in (in quantities and for the foreseeable future) before it is possible to predict what happens here in the future. But given the history of management, I like their chances of again providing a decent return from the current stock price.

Therefore, Paramount Resources remains a strong buy based upon management that has made a successful strategy work without using copious amounts of debt that the debt market no longer allows.

Risks

Well results and construction progress can disappoint at any time to set back the future of the company. This management has considerable experience at building and selling production. That lowers but does not eliminate risk.

Similarly, a debt free balance sheet also lowers investment risk because it is far easier for a comeback from a disappointment. What is interesting is that most "homeruns" I follow, like that sale, come from managements with a low debt or no debt strategy. That means they rely upon better acreage and better operations to produce those good results.

This goes against investors that look for highly leveraged companies that are likely to produce a "homerun." Those ideas usually fail because one setback is often fatal with a lot of leverage. Therefore, for every success, if any, there are generally a lot of sizable failures. I have covered more than a few failures when the then "new era" back in 2015 of lower oil prices caused a lot of failures under my profile.

It takes unusually good management to make a strategy that previously worked with using a lot of debt to now work using little to no debt. A loss of services of key management people could prove to be very hard to replace. It may therefore set back the future of the company.

Any upstream operation is exposed to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained commodity price downturn could well change the future outlook of the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rekor Systems Financial Situation Remains Complex, And The Name Is A Pass
data.description:
Rekor Systems (REKR) released 2Q25 results recently. The results are very negative, with sales stagnation and continued losses.

The company's financial situation, the main concern I had in my last article on the name, has not improved. Adjusted EBITDA losses for 2Q25 are higher than the company's cash reserves, and the ATM share offering is exhausted.

Importantly, the company maintained going-concern risk vocabulary in the 10-Q, something that should not be ignored. For that reason, I maintain a hold rating on Rekor Systems.

2Q25 results

Revenue stagnation

The results were not positive in general, especially considering that the financial situation requires urgent improvement. This is not a company that can wait for markets to develop, at least not with its current cost structure.

Revenues were flat YoY, signaling challenges with product adoption. For the 1H25 period, sales were actually down slightly, blamed on bad weather hindering the provision of services.

Gross margins were lower, implying also lower gross profits. The situation is not terrible, but it shows stagnation, not growth, which is what the company needs.

New contracts, but potentially not material

The company commented on the win of a contract with the Texas Department of Transportation, allowing municipalities to purchase its real-time control and alert systems. Although this could prove profitable (Texas is one of the largest US states by population, economy, and size, meaning roads), management was moderate in the call, saying that 'it is important to recognize that the expanded adoption across the rest of TxDOT's 25 districts is not a switch flip overnight. We expect the process of bringing additional districts on board to unfold over the coming quarters.'

Further, the size of the potential market was not revealed. The other two contracts won in the quarter (with another Texan authority and with a Sun Belt state authority) were for very small figures, less than $1.5 million each.

Some margin improvement, still large losses

Despite some improvement in cost reduction, the company remains fundamentally unprofitable. Adjusted EBITDA losses were almost $6 million for the quarter, or almost 50% of revenues ($12 million).

General and administrative expenses, potentially the ones with the lowest direct effect on the business operations, are the largest bucket, at about half of OpEx.

Financial situation is critical

The company's financial situation was very dire already when I last wrote about it. The company's cash losses were as much as $30 million per year back then, and the company only had an ATM share sale facility to finance it.

Rekor indeed made significant use of the facility, diluting shareholders. Diluted shares jumped 18 million (17% of the company's count) between December 2024 and June 2025.

The company had diluted shareholders by almost 20% by June 2025, yet cash remained flat at about $5 million. That level of cash is less than 1 quarter of adjusted EBITDA losses. CFO for the 1H25 period is negative by $15.7 million.

Between June and August, the company had issued another 4 million shares for $4.6 million in proceeds. This can potentially help the company sustain part of the adjusted EBITDA losses of one quarter, at current operating rates.

The ATM facility with Northland Securities was originally stated at a limit of $25 million, of which the company had already used $17.7 million as of June 2025, plus another $4.6 million in 3Q25 so far, for a total of $22.3 million. The above means the company's ATM is about to finish, and without additional financing arrangements, the company will face more serious financing challenges. Indeed, the company announced on the release that the 'ATM Offering has been terminated as part of strategic capital initiatives.'

Finally, and most concerning, is the fact that the company maintained the going-concern language in its 10-Q for the quarter. Quoting from the filing: 'Based on the company's current business plan assumptions and the expected cash burn rate, the Company believes that the existing cash is insufficient to fund its current level of operations. These factors raise substantial doubt regarding the company's ability to continue as a going concern for the next twelve months following the issuance of these unaudited condensed consolidated financial statements.'

Valuation is unattractive

Even assuming that Rekor Systems' financial situation was much better, say with $60 million in cash, I would not think that its current valuation was attractive.

The company has not shown growth in recent years, albeit (as commented in my previous article), most of that was inorganic via acquisitions. Now that the company is not acquiring more companies, growth has stalled and is even turning negative. Further, growth has not led to better profitability, with very negative operating margins and cash losses.

But more important than the operations is the company's dire financial situation. The company requires urgent financing, and in my opinion, the most probable outcome is a significant level of dilution to shareholders, more than has already occurred (more than 20% since the beginning of the year).

For that reason, I believe Rekor is an easy pass, and maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: It's Still Early For National Vision Holdings
data.description:
National Vision Holdings (NASDAQ:EYE) is a holding company for discount eyeglass stores. If you're not familiar with it, its most well-known store is America's Best, which has just refreshed its brand. In 2024, America's Best was the second-largest eyeglass store in the United States with 14.7 percent market share, behind EssilorLuxottica's (OTCPK:ESLOF)(OTCPK:ESLOY) LensCrafters with 14.8 percent of the market and ahead of Warby Parker (WRBY) with 7.2 percent market share. National Vision Holdings also owns Eyeglass World and Vista Optical.

Investors are currently interested in National Vision Holdings because it appears that a turnaround has begun after several years of declining share prices. The holding company's stock has already doubled this year after its first-quarter results impressed investors and showed them that its turnaround was gaining traction. Its second-quarter results provided more evidence that the turnaround was real. The company reported 5.9 percent adjusted same-store sales growth, beat EPS estimates by 38 percent, and raised guidance.

But this stock is no longer as much of a bargain as it was a few months ago. The turnaround has already been priced in to some extent. In April, you could buy this stock for $12 a share, but it has risen to $25 a share since then. Now, the question is whether National Vision Holdings can maintain its upward momentum. I think it can for several reasons. First of all, it's trading at lower multiples than its two main competitors on several metrics.

Value Comparison Versus Other Eyeglass Stores

National Vision Holdings' two main competitors are EssilorLuxottica and Warby Parker. National Vision Holdings and Warby Parker are currently unprofitable, so I decided to use EV/EBITDA to compare their earnings. National Vision Holdings has an EV/EBITDA of 25 versus 105 for Warby Parker and 20 for EssilorLuxottica. As for price-to-book value, National Vision Holdings has a P/B of 2.31 versus 9.12 for Warby Parker and 3.32 for EssilorLuxottica. And National Vision Holdings has a P/S of 1.04 versus 4.06 for Warby Parker and 4.61 for EssilorLuxottica.

It's arguable that Warby Parker deserves a higher valuation because of its growth potential. Warby Parker has 300 stores right now and plans to expand to 900, and it also has a partnership with Target to add shops inside its stores. National Vision currently has 1,300 stores, so even though it's still adding new stores, it can't grow as fast. It's also closing some of its underperforming stores. In overall terms, it plans to open 32 stores and close 20 stores by the end of 2025 for a net increase of 12 stores, according to the CEO on its second-quarter earnings call.

As for partnerships, National Vision used to have an agreement with Walmart (WMT) where it operated 231 eyeglass shops inside Walmart stores. The stores maintained this partnership for more than 30 years, but Walmart decided not to renew it in 2023, and the partnership ended in 2024. National Vision also has a partnership with Fred Meyer, a Kroger (KR) regional brand in the Pacific Northwest, to operate Vista Optical stores inside Fred Meyer grocery stores. It's not clear how many Vista Optical stores there are, but this doesn't seem to be a large chain.

Across several metrics, National Vision Holdings still has a lower valuation than EssilorLuxottica, which is a much larger company and the clear leader in the eyeglass store market. EssilorLuxottica is a global company with 18,000 stores, including 3,820 stores in North America, that operates mass-market premium brands such as LensCrafters and Sunglass Hut. Both National Vision Holdings' shareholders and Warby Parker's shareholders believe that their companies can capture some of EssilorLuxottica's market share by selling eyeglasses at lower prices. But that's not the only opportunity available to this eyeglass store.

Moving Upmarket

In its second-quarter report, National Vision Holdings explained that its stores weren't just attracting discount shoppers anymore. According to the company, it's now attracting more customers who earn between $75k and $100k, and it's also stocking more brand-name eyeglass frames priced above $99 because insurance will pay an average of $130 for frames. Additionally, the company is also starting to stock AI glasses, including both Meta's Ray-Ban glasses and Nuance's audio glasses.

According to The Verge, EssilorLuxottica has already sold 2 million pairs of the Meta Ray-Ban glasses and plans to produce 10 million pairs of the AI glasses per year by 2026. Right now, several stores are selling these glasses for $299. Warby Parker also has a partnership with Google to make AI glasses, but they won't be ready in 2025.

As for the Nuance audio glasses, according to Lifehacker, they're priced at $1,200, but that's still less expensive than a hearing aid for $5,000 to $7,000. The Nuance audio glasses are also an EssilorLuxottica product. While investors may have seen news stories about EssilorLuxottica and its AI eyeglasses, it might not be as well known that National Vision Holdings is selling them at its America's Best stores as well.

Risk Factors

One major risk factor for National Vision Holdings, as well as its competitors Warby Parker and EssilorLuxottica, is tariffs. While the company has revised its guidance to include the expected impact of tariffs and it has started sourcing parts from other Asian countries besides China to avoid high tariffs on Chinese imports, there's a possibility that it could still be impacted by an unwelcome surprise in this area.

Another risk factor is that if the economy weakens, low-income customers might not have enough cash to buy new prescription eyeglasses. And if they can afford glasses, they're likely to buy lower-priced glasses that won't be as profitable for the company. This risk factor also applies to the other eyeglass stores, of course. National Vision Holdings is addressing the risk by updating its brand to appeal to wealthier customers who can pay cash for brand-name glasses, and it's also focusing on managed care customers who can pay for their glasses with insurance.

Valuation

Right now, analysts' one-year price target for National Vision Holdings is $29, which is 16 percent higher than its current price of $25. Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts both rate it a buy. The other SA analysts who follow this stock also think a turnaround is currently in progress, but they recommended buying it at lower prices. So I'll explain why it's still worth buying at $25.

National Vision Holdings has beaten earnings estimates 8 times in two years. It beat estimates by 4 cents per share last quarter and 3 cents in the quarter before that. It's in the middle of a turnaround and is likely to continue improving its operations. If you estimate that analysts' current estimates for National Vision Holdings' quarterly earnings are 4 cents too low, then it would earn $0.97 per share next year instead of $0.83. If its forward P/E stays the same at 41, that's $40 per share, a 60 percent premium over the current price.

If you want to consider a more speculative argument, you could argue that since Warby Parker and EssilorLuxottica both have P/S ratios above 4, National Vision Holdings could eventually trade at that valuation by improving its margins as well. If you divide 4 by the company's current P/S of 1.04 and multiply that by $25, you get a price of $96 per share.

That might sound excessive to value investors, but tech companies often trade at high P/S multiples because of their high gross margins. All three of these eyeglass stores have gross margins of over 50 percent, so they're more profitable than other types of stores and more like tech companies. And National Vision Holdings could also increase earnings per share by adding fewer new stores, so it may have higher earning power than its financial results suggest. It's much easier to justify a target price of $40 per share going by its recent earnings, of course.

Conclusion

Even though National Vision Holdings' share price has doubled in recent months, this rally may not be over. This company's turnaround is still ongoing, and it has potential catalysts ahead if pricey AI glasses gain popularity. It's also implementing a brand refresh for America's Best that could help it attract wealthier customers. Additionally, peer valuations imply that its true earnings power might be much higher in the future than analysts realize, although that's speculative.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Devon Energy: Strong Acquisitions, Economic Headwinds, Hold On
data.description:
Devon Energy (NYSE:DVN), is a United States oil and gas producer with multiple basins in their portfolio, operating strictly in the upstream oil industry. The company finds itself at a crossroads: the macro environment for oil remains subdued, with uncertain WTI crude projections, leaving earnings and free cash flow possibly capped in the near term. However, the company has taken meaningful steps to strengthen its portfolio through acquisitions and agreements, which will bolster their production levels. While these moves support long-term value and operational scale, current market conditions are limiting. I rate DVN a hold.

The stock is down about 55% since its high in late 2022. In the crazy YTD the market has had, DVN has eked out a 2.93% gain. The large cap firm has a market cap of $21.44 billion, which is on the smaller side of many peers. Their current dividend is 2.72% but has fluctuated largely over the years.

Natural Gas Deal

About a week ago, DVN came to an agreement with Centrica, owner of British Gas, on a long-term natural gas deal. It involves DVN supplying the equivalent of 5 Liquified Natural Gas (LNG) cargoes per year beginning in 2028. This will total 50,000 MMBtu per day and will be indexed to the European Gas Hub price (TTF). This is a huge, long haul purchase agreement for DVN and provides them with some international price exposure, which they currently lack.

Grayson Mill Acquisition

In the Fall of 2024, DVN completed the acquisition of Grayson Mill Energy’s Williston Basin at around $5 billion. With this transaction, DVN adds 307,000 net acres to their basin, and an estimated 100,000 boepd. It is noted that there are up to 500 new well locations that have been undrilled, signaling at the future potential the land holds. Based on current oil prices, the transaction could lead to a ballpark of $1.5 billion in additional revenue.

Spending vs Production

Q2 net production 841,000 barrels per day, beating expectations and up 18.9% YoY. Natural gas liquids gained 21.9% and oil was higher by 15.5% YoY. This was done by reducing capital spend by 400 million, highlighting their improving capital efficiency. At the start of the year DVN expected $4.1 billion in capex spending and now anticipates about $3.7 billion.

The increase in production numbers took into account the added barrels produced from Grayson Mill at the Williston Basin. Where I am concerned is that gross operated wells tied-in decreased YoY, and largely QoQ, as the wells from Grayson Mill are not taken into consideration yet. Wells tied-in shows how many wells are successfully operating and ultimately is an indicator as to the direction future revenue is headed. 3 of the original 4 basins are down on operating wells, and although the Grayson Mill addition will help, it leaves you wondering why the drop in other basins. The push for lower spend on capex, and softening oil prices are likely driving DVN to pull out of some operations. Strategically I’m not saying that is a good or bad decision, but at the end of the day results in less net production.

Oil Outlook & Connection

DVN is extremely correlated to the price of crude oil, which I find unfavorable in today’s market. With Brent and WTI both priced in the mid $60s per barrel currently, forecasts consistently estimate $50-$55 moving into 2026. The EIA projects that supply growth will be greater than demand, and global uncertainty remains high. As an upstream business, DVN gets their core revenue from selling oil and natural gas. The lower the price, simply the last revenue they will have. This also makes margins tight and scalability challenging.

You can see above the chart for WTI crude correlation with their quarterly revenue and growth. Oil hit a peak in 2014 and 2022. DVN revenue and growth also hit peaks in 2014 and 2022. The connection goes much more in depth and is my biggest worry for DVN moving forward. Oil hit its pinnacle in June 2022 and has softened since. It’s no coincidence that earnings for DVN hit their highs in Q2 and Q3 of 2022 at $2.59 and $2.18 respectively. Revenue on those same two quarters were $5.63 and $5.43 billion.

In Q2 2025, adjusted EPS was $0.84 and revenue was $4.28 billion. It’s a double-edged sword, but with the global economic and political state, mixed with the EIA’s forecast on oil prices, I don’t think the next year or so is going to be generous to DVN on the earning and sales front.

Conclusion

A bright spot for DVN is that they finished their full acquisition of Cotton Draw Midstream earlier this month. This was a strong strategic move, I like the midstream space, especially for oil investors during periods of lower prices.

Valuation is also a strength for DVN, with a P/E of 7.61, almost half the sector average, and a huge discount compared to the broader market.

Ultimately, the relationship DVN carries with oil price is heavy and a concern in the next year or two from my perspective. I don’t think it warrants a sell rating, especially with that valuation, but I just can’t see their being much growth by the numbers in the next year if oil prices sit where there are now or head even lower. History repeats itself, and when oil prices drop, DVN stock doesn’t fair as well. Hold on tight.

data.title: Portland General Electric Benefits From Hydropower
data.description:
Portland General Electric (NYSE:POR) is an integrated electric utility company serving 950,000 customers in fifty-one Oregon cities. It generates, purchases wholesale, transmits, distributes, and retails electricity.

This $4.7 billion market cap company offers a 4.9% dividend, which is more than the 10-year Treasury rate.Some investors may also like its low beta of 0.57 and top-notch (highest possible) governance scores.

The future drop(s) in interest rates just telegraphed by the Federal Reserve helps Portland General and all utilities with potentially lower future financing costs and in competing for dividend-seeking investors.

Portland General is seeking approval of a holding company reorganization. According to the company, this means placing a non-operating corporate entity over the company's existing structure to allow forming a subsidiary to hold existing and future transmission assets. In the Q2 2025 investor presentation, the company estimated an effective date for the reorganization of mid-2026.

I rank Portland General Electric as a buy for dividend seekers due to its dividend above the 10-year US Treasury rate, its ability to resell some of its power on a wholesale basis, and its range of power sources, including particularly hydropower but also natural gas, coal, and purchased power from a variety of sources.

Second Quarter 2025 Results and Guidance

For the second quarter of 2025, Portland General Electric reported net income of $62 million or $0.56/share. This compares to net income of $72 million or $0.69/share for Q2 2024.

For the first six months of 2025, net income was $162 million or $1.47/share. This compares to net income of $182 million or $1.77/share for the first six months of 2024.

According to the company, the second quarter “reflected significant demand growth from data center customers, driving 16.5% industrial load growth quarter-over-quarter.”

The company’s report shows that for the first six months of 2025 (and 2024), the biggest primary energy sources for electricity generation were from POR’s own natural gas generation, followed by the total of its generated and purchased hydropower.

In the first half of 2025, and similarly for 2024, the company sold about 30% of its total system load on a wholesale basis to other customers.

In the Q2 2025 investor call, CEO Maria Pope outlined the company’s priorities as, “First, investing in customer-driven clean energy goals; second, working to keep customer prices as low as possible; third, supporting data center and high-tech growth and the region's economic development; fourth, reducing risk through operational execution, system hardening and wildfire policies; and fifth, promoting an investable energy future for Oregon, updating our corporate structure and aligning legislative and regulatory policies.”

Portland General expects to make capital expenditures of between $1.15 billion and $1.44 billion annually between 2025 and 2029 to build generation, distribution, and transmission projects. For example, the company recently energized 475 MW of battery storage.

Portland General Electric affirmed full-year 2025 adjusted earnings per share (EPS) guidance at $3.13-$3.33/diluted share. Per the company’s Q2 2025 earnings news release, this estimate is subject to the following assumptions:

• “An increase in energy deliveries between 2.5% and 3.5%, weather adjusted;

• Execution of power cost and financing plans;

• Execution of operating cost controls;

• Normal temperatures in its utility service territory;

• Hydro conditions for the year that reflect current estimates;

• Wind generation based on five years of historical levels or forecast studies when historical data is not available;

• Normal thermal plant operations;

• Operating and maintenance expense between $795 million and $815 million which includes approximately $135 million of wildfire, vegetation management, deferral amortization and other expenses that are offset in other income statement lines;

• Depreciation and amortization expense between $550 million and $575 million;

• Effective tax rate of 15% to 20%;

• Cash from operations of $900 to $1,000 million;

• Capital expenditures of $1,215 million; and

• Average construction work in progress balance of $595 million.”

Macro

At its meeting on August 22, 2025, in Jackson Hole, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell suggested a policy realignment, with a likely move to cut rates in the near future. One or more rate cuts would make dividend stocks, including utilities, more attractive, both for a) financing their significant debt loads and b) competing for return-seeking investors.

Significant data center growth for artificial intelligence (AI) and its resulting big appetite for electricity continues within the utility sector and, for some companies, behind the meter (self-generated electricity, however, with backups for reliability).

The passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB)—and the sunsetting at the end of 2027 of many renewable energy tax credits--has led companies like Portland General to request a “refresh” of terms from sponsors of proposed renewable projects.

However, it is worth noting that tax credits for energy storage projects and for hydropower are extended longer, through the end of 2033.

Between AI, tariff negotiations, reshoring, and changes in federal energy policy to increase both energy supply and demand, the economic environment for utilities is considerably different from that of a year ago.

Portland General’s Electrical Generation

Portland General Electric is headquartered in Portland, Oregon. It owns 3570 megawatts of electricity-generating capacity. This includes operation of six thermal (natural gas and coal) plants, four wind farms, and seven hydroelectric facilities. It also owns a 1269 circuit-mile electric transmission system.

Be aware that the considerable volume of purchased hydropower shown in the graph above is not included in the output or capacity of the plants listed below. The generation plants POR owns are:

Natural gas:

*Beaver - 513 MW - Clatskanie, Oregon

*Carty - 426 MW - Boardman, Oregon

*Coyote Springs - 257 MW - Boardman, Oregon

*Port Westward Unit 1 - 403 MW - Clatskanie, Oregon

*Port Westward Unit 2 - 219 MW - Clatskanie, Oregon

Coal:

*Colstrip - 296 MW - Colstrip, Montana (20% ownership)

Hydro:

*Clackamas River, West Side Hydro - Estacada, Oregon, 4 units, 169 MW

*Willamette River, West Linn, Oregon, 1 unit, 18 MW

*Deschutes River, Pelton Round Butte - Madras, Oregon, 2 units, 244 MW

(co-owned with Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs)

Wind:

*Biglow Canyon Wind Farm- 450 MW - Sherman Co., Oregon

*Tucannon Wind Farm- 267 MW - Dayton, Washington

*Wheatridge Renewable Energy - 100 MW - Morrow Co., Oregon (co-owned with NextEra (NEE) )

*Clearwater Wind - 208 MW - Miles City, Montana (co-owned with NextEra)

The diagram below shows the mix for Portland General’s entire system load, retail and wholesale.

POR buys and sells power as needed. Specifically, it participates in the Western Energy Imbalance Market (EIM) for dispatching the lowest-cost power for short-term customer needs. This helps optimize use of renewably-sourced power in the region.

Dispatchable Hydropower

An energy source long key and somewhat unique to the Pacific Northwest’s electricity supply is hydropower. As illustrated above, purchased and generated hydropower accounted for 31% of Portland General Electric’s supply in the first half of 2025.

It is no accident that a picture of the nearby Columbia River is featured on the investor page of the company’s website. More specifically, the Pacific Northwest is served by the Federal Columbia River Power System. According to the Bonneville Power Administration (BPA) (with the Bonneville Dam itself just upstream from Portland), hydroelectric dams in the Northwest produce more electricity than any other North American river system. Per the BPA, “The FCRPS is a collaborative operation among BPA as the power marketer, and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and Bureau of Reclamation as the plant operators.”

Initially, data centers prioritized non-hydrocarbon power (nuclear, renewable, hydro) for their electricity supply. However, because data centers require so much power—and 24/7 reliable power--this is evolving to self-generation or matching to specific plants (often with natural gas first, nuclear longer-term, and renewables backed up by battery storage as a third option.)

The need for reliable, dispatchable power generally has also led to a focus in the US on keeping coal plants open, especially in the PJM (mid-Atlantic) and MISO regional grids, for areas like those served by Black Hills Corporation (BKH) and MDU Resources (MDU).

Across the US, reliability and dispatchability (and affordability) have become more prominent and desirable attributes compared to the previous preference for only renewables (which, as intermittent sources, require battery storage or 1.1:1 backup with natural gas for reliability). Hydropower at the scale available to Portland General and the Pacific Northwest easily meets the criteria of dispatchability.

Different from coal or nuclear, hydropower obviously depends year to year on regional rainfall and snowpack.

Natural Gas Prices

The graph below shows a 5-95 confidence interval projection of future natural gas prices through the end of 2026 and illustrates an expected upward trend. The Henry Hub natural gas price closed at $2.70/MMBTU for September 2025 deliveries on August 22, 2025.

Regulators and Competition

As an electric utility, Portland General does not have direct competitors. However, it has oversight from and reporting responsibilities to the Oregon Public Utilities Commission (OPUC).

In rate cases, Portland General Electric answers to a wide variety of customer-stakeholders. The company is also subject to normal market pressures for its fuel sources and to changes in demand for its electricity generation, transmission, and distribution.

An important supplier to Portland General is the FCRPS, which as noted above, is a collaboration between the BPA, the US Army Corps of Engineers, and the Bureau of Reclamation.

Portland General competes for industrial customers and specifically data center customers with utilities in other regions and with the tech companies themselves. Tech companies can either buy electricity from utilities, generate it themselves, or both.

Governance

On August 1, 2025, Institutional Shareholder Services ranked Portland General’s overall governance as a perfect 1, with sub-scores of audit (1), board (1), shareholder rights (1), and compensation (1). In this ranking, 1 indicates lower governance risk and 10 indicates higher governance risk.

Insiders own a small 0.54% of the stock. On July 31, 2025, 4.2% of the floated stock was shorted.

Portland General’s beta is 0.57: like most utilities, its stock does not fluctuate as sharply as the overall market.

As of June 30, 2025, most of the company’s stock was held by institutions, some of which represent index fund investments that match the overall market. The top five institutional holders are BlackRock with 13.4%, Vanguard with 11.5%, ATLAS Infrastructure Partners (UK) Ltd. (5.0%), Fuller & Thaler Asset Management (4.7%), and State Street with 4.15%.

Financial and Stock Highlights

Portland General’s market capitalization is $4.72 billion at the August 22, 2025, stock closing price of $43.08/share.

Trailing twelve months (TTM) EPS is $2.71, for a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 15.9.The averages of analysts' projected 2025 and 2026 EPS are $3.23 and $3.40, respectively, for a forward price-to-earnings ratio range of 12.7-13.3.

The 52-week price range is $39.55-$49.85 per share, so the most recent closing price is 86% of the 52-week high. The closing price is 93% of the company's one-year target of $46.36/share.

TTM returns on assets and equity are 2.7% and 8.0%, respectively.

On June 30, 2025, Portland General had $8.83 billion in liabilities, including $4.73 billion of long-term debt ($68 million current maturities) and $12.68 billion in assets, resulting in a liability-to-asset ratio of 70%.

Mean analyst rating is 2.8, essentially “hold,” from thirteen analysts.

The dividend of $2.10/share yields 4.9%. This is more than the 10-year Treasury rate shown below, currently 4.26%.

Notes on Valuation

The company’s enterprise value (EV) is $9.6 billion, and its EV/EBITDA ratio is 9.1. This is below the preferred ratio of 10 or less for this metric and so suggests a modest bargain.

Book value per share of $35.16 is less than the market price, indicating positive market sentiment.

Seeking Alpha Factor Grades are:

Valuation: A

Growth: B

Profitability: C

Momentum: D

Revisions: C-

Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades are:

Safety: B+

Growth: B-

Yield: A-

Consistency: A-

Positive and Negative Risks

Positive risks include Portland General’s well-established, affordable and dispatchable (24/7) energy sources, particularly hydropower from the Federal Columbia River Power System, as well as natural gas.

A general positive risk for all utilities is the potential for interest rate cuts.

The company’s electric generation revenues depend on regional economic growth in its service area including competition for data center growth, the Oregon state regulatory environment, and regional population growth. One regulatory risk is that the proposed restructuring into a holding company may not be approved quickly.

Negative risks include hydropower’s reliance on seasonal snowfall and rainfall, higher natural gas prices as gas demand increases, and increasing costs of construction.

Market risk overall continues to the downside since equity markets have been hitting new all-time highs.

Recommendations for Portland General Electric Company

I recommend Portland General Electric Company as a buy, particularly to dividend investors, given its 4.9% dividend.

Investors looking to diversify into lower-beta stocks or for whom governance is a key factor may like Portland General Electric’s beta of 0.57 and its superior governance score of 1. The company has benefited from data center development. In addition, its electric generation and purchased power have good diversification across hydropower, natural gas, renewables, and coal.

I own shares of Portland General Electric Company.
